title: JAMB Subject Combination: How to Choose the Right Subjects for Your Course
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You are filling out your JAMB registration form. You reach the section where you must select your four UTME subjects. English Language is already locked in. You now have to choose three more. The subjects you select will determine which courses you are eligible for at every Nigerian university. A wrong choice here can lock you out of your dream course, and correcting it after registration is difficult and sometimes impossible. Here is how to choose the right UTME subject combination for your intended career.

The Four-Subject Rule

Every UTME candidate takes exactly four subjects. English Language is compulsory. The remaining three must be relevant to your intended course of study. The relevance is determined by the JAMB brochure, which lists the required and accepted subjects for every course at every Nigerian university. If your subject combination does not match what the brochure requires for your intended course, your application will be rejected, regardless of how high your score is.

The brochure is published annually and is available on the JAMB website. It is the authoritative source. Do not rely on what your friends tell you, what your teachers remember from previous years, or what you read on a forum. Requirements can and do change. Check the current brochure for the current year. A few minutes of verification can prevent a year of regret.

Each course has required subjects and sometimes optional or recommended subjects. A required subject is non-negotiable. If Medicine requires Biology, Chemistry, and Physics, you must take all three. An optional subject gives you a choice. If a course lists Economics or Commerce as options, you can choose either. The key is to ensure that your combination includes all the required subjects and that your optional subjects are from the approved list.

Subject Combinations for Popular Courses

Medicine and Surgery: English, Biology, Chemistry, Physics. This combination is non-negotiable. All four subjects are required. There are no alternatives. If you do not take all four, you cannot study Medicine at any Nigerian university.

Engineering (all branches): English, Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry. Mathematics and Physics are the core requirements. Chemistry is required by most universities. Some universities accept Technical Drawing or Further Mathematics as a fourth subject instead of Chemistry for specific engineering branches, but the safe choice is Chemistry. It keeps all engineering options open.

Law: English, Literature in English, Government, and one other subject from the Arts or Social Sciences, typically CRS, IRS, Economics, or History. Literature in English is the most important elective for Law. It is required by almost every university. Government is also widely required or recommended.

Accounting and Business Administration: English, Mathematics, Economics, and one other Commercial or Social Science subject such as Commerce, Government, or Financial Accounting. Mathematics is essential. Economics is the core social science for business courses. The fourth subject can be chosen based on what you are strongest in.

Computer Science: English, Mathematics, Physics, and one other subject, typically Chemistry, Further Mathematics, or Economics. Mathematics and Physics are the core requirements. The fourth subject varies by university. Check the brochure for your target institutions.

Mass Communication: English, Literature in English, Government, and one other Arts or Social Science subject. English and Literature are the most relevant. Mass Communication involves extensive writing and analysis of media texts, and your performance in English and Literature signals your readiness for that work.

Nursing and Pharmacy: English, Biology, Chemistry, Physics. The requirements are identical to Medicine. The science combination is the gateway to all medical and health science courses.

What to Do If You Are Undecided

If you have not yet decided on a specific course, choose a subject combination that keeps the maximum number of doors open. The science combination, English, Biology, Chemistry, Physics, qualifies you for Medicine, Engineering, Pharmacy, Computer Science, and most science-based courses. It is the most versatile combination available. The social science combination, English, Mathematics, Economics, Government, qualifies you for Accounting, Business Administration, Economics, Political Science, and related courses. The arts combination, English, Literature, Government, CRS or IRS, qualifies you for Law, Mass Communication, and humanities courses.

If you are torn between science and social science, the science combination is the safer default. A science student can switch to social science courses more easily than a social science student can switch to science courses. The science subjects are prerequisites for science courses. The social science subjects are not prerequisites for most science courses. Keeping the science door open preserves more options.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

The most common mistake is selecting subjects based on what you think will be easy rather than what your intended course requires. A student who wants to study Medicine but is afraid of Physics and selects Economics instead has disqualified themselves from Medicine. The short-term comfort of an easier subject is not worth the long-term cost of a closed career path.

Another common mistake is not checking the specific requirements for your target universities. While most universities have similar requirements, there are variations. One university may require Further Mathematics for Engineering while another does not. One may accept Civic Education as a social science subject while another does not. The brochure lists requirements by university and by course. Use it.

Some students select subjects based on what their friends are taking or what is popular at their school. This is not a strategy. Your subject combination should be determined by your career goals and the published requirements, not by social pressure. Your friends are not the ones who will live with the consequences of your choices.

The Bottom Line

Your JAMB subject combination is one of the most consequential decisions of your academic career. English is compulsory. The remaining three subjects must be chosen based on the published requirements for your intended course in the JAMB brochure. If you are undecided, choose a combination that keeps the most doors open, with the science combination being the most versatile. Check the specific requirements for at least three target universities before finalizing your registration. The time you invest in choosing the right subjects now is an investment in your future options.
